description: PowerScore-derived LR taxonomy: axis-1 question types, axis-2 reasoning sub-skills, trap catalog. Stable data contract for WP-1 and downstream work packages. D-SR13.

axis1_question_types.description: Deductive inference from the stimulus; the correct answer must be true given the facts.
Identify the primary conclusion of the argument.
Identify the claim two speakers disagree about (or agree on).
Describe how the argument proceeds (abstract structure, not content).
Identify and name the logical flaw in the argument.
Find the argument most similar in logical structure; Parallel Flaw variant targets the same flaw.
Identify the choice that most strengthens the argument.
Find the assumption the argument depends on (negate it and the argument fails).
Find the choice that, if true, guarantees the conclusion follows.
Identify the choice that most undermines the argument.
Identify which piece of information would most help evaluate the argument's strength.
Find the choice that explains the apparent contradiction or surprising result.
Identify an underlying principle OR apply a given principle to a specific case.

axis1_subtypes.description: Role of a Statement variant — what role does this statement play?
Parallel Flaw — find the argument with the same logical flaw
Identify an underlying principle from examples
Apply a stated principle to a specific case

axis2_reasoning_subskills.name: Conclusion / Premise Identification
axis2_reasoning_subskills.description: Identify which statement is the conclusion vs. premises. Misidentification cascades into assumption, flaw, and strengthen/weaken failures.

axis2_reasoning_subskills.name: Conditional Reasoning
axis2_reasoning_subskills.description: Sufficient/necessary conditions, if/then chains, contrapositive. The most common source of flaw questions.

axis2_reasoning_subskills.name: Causal Reasoning
axis2_reasoning_subskills.description: Arguments that assert causation. Strengthen/weaken levers: alternate cause, reverse causation, coincidence, common cause.

axis2_reasoning_subskills.name: Prephrasing
axis2_reasoning_subskills.description: Generating a prediction of the correct answer before reading choices (K.1 generation step). Reduces contender-lock trap.

axis2_reasoning_subskills.name: Formal-Logic Quantifiers
axis2_reasoning_subskills.description: Inference rules for all/most/some/none. Rare on post-2024 LSAT; treated as a separate sub-skill to track.

axis2_reasoning_subskills.name: Abstraction
axis2_reasoning_subskills.description: Stripping content to see pure logical form — required for Parallel Reasoning and abstract Flaw questions. The hardest cluster.

trap_catalog.description: Finite enumerable catalog of named traps. Two sub-catalogs: argument flaws (in stimulus) and distractor/answer-choice traps. Tag: trap::<slug>.

argument_flaws.name: Sufficient / Necessary Confusion
argument_flaws.description: Conflates 'if A then B' (A sufficient for B) with 'if B then A' (B sufficient for A, A necessary for B). Most common flaw on the exam.

argument_flaws.name: Correlation vs. Causation
argument_flaws.description: Observes correlation between two phenomena and incorrectly infers one causes the other.

argument_flaws.name: Circular Reasoning
argument_flaws.description: The conclusion restates or presupposes a premise; also called 'begging the question.'

argument_flaws.name: Equivocation
argument_flaws.description: A key term shifts meaning (or is ambiguous) between its use in the premise and the conclusion.

argument_flaws.name: Part-to-Whole / Whole-to-Part
argument_flaws.description: What is true of parts must be true of the whole, or vice versa (composition/division fallacy).

argument_flaws.name: Hasty Generalization
argument_flaws.description: Draws a broad conclusion from an unrepresentative or too-small sample.

argument_flaws.name: False Dichotomy
argument_flaws.description: Presents only two options as if exhaustive when other alternatives exist.

argument_flaws.name: Straw Man
argument_flaws.description: Misrepresents an opponent's position then attacks the misrepresentation.

argument_flaws.name: Ad Hominem
argument_flaws.description: Attacks the source or speaker rather than the argument.

argument_flaws.name: Appeal to Inappropriate Authority
argument_flaws.description: Cites an authority without sufficient warrant for the specific domain or claim.

argument_flaws.name: False Analogy
argument_flaws.description: Draws an analogy between two situations that differ in a relevant respect.

argument_flaws.name: Scope Shift
argument_flaws.description: The conclusion concerns a different entity, property, or time frame than what the premises support.

distractor_traps.name: Half-True
distractor_traps.description: The choice is partially correct but incomplete or imprecise; passes a quick read but fails on inspection.

distractor_traps.name: Too Extreme
distractor_traps.description: Uses absolute language (always, never, all, none) where the stimulus is hedged or probabilistic.

distractor_traps.name: Out of Scope
distractor_traps.description: Introduces a concept, entity, or comparison not present in the stimulus.

distractor_traps.name: Contradicts Text
distractor_traps.description: Directly contradicts a stated or clearly inferable fact from the stimulus.

distractor_traps.name: Wrong Direction
distractor_traps.description: Strengthens the argument when the task is to weaken it, or vice versa.

distractor_traps.name: Reversal
distractor_traps.description: The choice itself confuses sufficient and necessary conditions (distinct from the stimulus flaw F01).

distractor_traps.name: Irrelevant Comparison
distractor_traps.description: Compares entities or groups that are not at issue in the argument.

tagging_rules: Every LSAT Item note carries exactly one type:: tag.
Every LSAT Item note carries one or more skill:: tags.
Wrong answer choices are tagged with a trap:: slug in the TrapChoice<X> fields.
The stimulus flaw (for type::flaw questions) is tagged in TrapTag.
Every LSAT Skill note carries exactly one identity tag: a type::, skill::, or trap:: tag.
Tag strings are kebab-case lowercase. No spaces inside a tag value.
